items.myth: Logistic regression predicts continuous numeric outcomes like linear regression.
items.reality: It predicts P(y=1|x) in (0, 1) and assigns discrete classes via a threshold. The regression in the name means the log-odds are modeled as a linear function of features — the task is classification.
items.example: ŷ = 0.73 means 73% estimated chance of class 1, not a dollar amount or temperature.

items.myth: Sigmoid and BCE are optional add-ons — any loss works for binary labels.
items.reality: Sigmoid + BCE is the standard pairing: sigmoid ensures valid Bernoulli probabilities; BCE is the negative log-likelihood for those probabilities. MSE on 0/1 labels is not the correct probabilistic model.
items.example: Training with MSE on labels often yields poor probabilities and weak gradients compared to BCE.

items.myth: If training accuracy is high, the model is well calibrated.
items.reality: Accuracy at one threshold ignores probability quality. A model can be accurate at 0.5 while assigning ŷ = 0.51 to most positives — high BCE and poor calibration for risk use cases.
items.example: Two models with 90% accuracy: one gives ŷ ≈ 0.9 on correct positives, the other ŷ ≈ 0.55 — same accuracy, different BCE.

items.myth: Logistic regression cannot handle multiple classes.
items.reality: Binary logistic regression generalizes to multi-class via softmax over K linear scores with categorical cross-entropy — the K=2 case reduces to one sigmoid and BCE.
items.example: sklearn LogisticRegression(multi_class='multinomial') trains softmax + CE for digit classification.
